title: Testing Your Chatbot (User Acceptance Testing)
description: Learn how to systematically test your chatbot using User Acceptance Testing (UAT) with formal test cases to ensure it meets requirements.

content.text:
## Learning Objectives
- Understand what User Acceptance Testing (UAT) is and why it matters
- Create formal test cases with expected inputs and outputs
- Systematically test your chatbot against requirements
- Document test results and identify issues
- Debug common problems

---

## What is User Acceptance Testing (UAT)?

**User Acceptance Testing** is when you test your software to make sure it does what it's supposed to do from a user's perspective.

### Why UAT Matters

| Without UAT | With UAT |
|-------------|----------|
| "It works on my computer!" | "I've tested all the requirements" |
| Users find bugs | You find bugs first |
| Embarrassing mistakes | Professional quality |
| Guessing if it works | Evidence it works |

> 💡 **Key Idea**: UAT asks "Does this software do what the user needs?" - not just "Does the code run?"

content.question: What is the main goal of User Acceptance Testing (UAT)?
options: To check if the code compiles without errors
To verify the software meets user requirements and expectations
To test how fast the application runs
To check if the code follows style guidelines
content.explanation: UAT focuses on verifying that the software does what users need it to do. It tests from the user's perspective, ensuring requirements are met before the software is considered complete.

content.text:
## What is a Test Case?

A **test case** is a set of steps and expected results that you use to verify a specific feature works correctly.

### The Anatomy of a Test Case

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Test ID** | Unique identifier | TC-001 |
| **Description** | What you're testing | Test normal greeting message |
| **Preconditions** | What must be true before testing | App is running, user is on chat page |
| **Test Steps** | What actions to perform | 1. Type "Hello" 2. Click Send |
| **Test Data** | The specific input | "Hello" |
| **Expected Result** | What SHOULD happen | Bot responds with a greeting |
| **Actual Result** | What DID happen | (fill in during testing) |
| **Status** | Pass or Fail | ✅ Pass / ❌ Fail |

### Example Test Case

```
Test ID: TC-001
Description: Normal greeting message
Preconditions: App running, chat page loaded
Test Steps:
  1. Type "Hello" in the message input
  2. Click the Send button
Test Data: "Hello"
Expected Result: Bot responds with a greeting (e.g., "Hi there!", "Hello!", "Hey!")
Actual Result: ____________
Status: ⬜ Pass / ⬜ Fail
```

## Your Chatbot Requirements

Before we test, let's list what your chatbot MUST do:

### Functional Requirements

| ID | Requirement |
|----|-------------|
| FR-1 | User can send a message and receive a response |
| FR-2 | Bot responds with relevant answers to common questions |
| FR-3 | Empty messages display an error message |
| FR-4 | Messages over 500 characters display an error message |
| FR-5 | Crisis keywords trigger helpline information |
| FR-6 | A disclaimer is visible to users |

### Non-Functional Requirements

| ID | Requirement |
|----|-------------|
| NFR-1 | Interface is visually clear and easy to use |
| NFR-2 | User messages appear on the right (blue) |
| NFR-3 | Bot messages appear on the left (grey) |
| NFR-4 | Chat scrolls automatically for new messages |

> 💡 **Tip**: You can add your own requirements if you've added extra features!

content.question: What is the difference between a Functional Requirement and a Non-Functional Requirement?
options: Functional requirements are optional, non-functional are mandatory
Functional requirements describe WHAT the system does, non-functional describe HOW it performs
Functional requirements are for testers, non-functional are for developers
There is no difference, they are the same thing
content.explanation: Functional requirements describe specific behaviors or functions (what the system DOES), like 'user can send a message'. Non-functional requirements describe qualities or characteristics (HOW it performs), like 'interface is visually clear' or 'response time is under 2 seconds'.

content.text:
## Complete Test Case Template

Here are the test cases you'll use to test your chatbot:

### TC-001: Normal Message Response
```
Test ID: TC-001
Requirement: FR-1, FR-2
Description: User sends a normal greeting and receives a response
Preconditions: App running at localhost:5000
Test Steps:
  1. Navigate to http://localhost:5000
  2. Type "Hello" in the input field
  3. Click Send or press Enter
Test Data: "Hello"
Expected Result: Bot displays a relevant greeting response
Actual Result: ____________
Status: ⬜ Pass / ⬜ Fail
```

### TC-002: Empty Message Handling
```
Test ID: TC-002
Requirement: FR-3
Description: Empty messages are rejected with an error
Preconditions: App running
Test Steps:
  1. Leave the input field empty
  2. Click Send
Test Data: "" (empty string)
Expected Result: Error message "Please enter a message!"
Actual Result: ____________
Status: ⬜ Pass / ⬜ Fail
```

### TC-003: Message Length Validation
```
Test ID: TC-003
Requirement: FR-4
Description: Messages over 500 characters are rejected
Preconditions: App running
Test Steps:
  1. Type or paste a message with 501+ characters
  2. Click Send
Test Data: [501 'a' characters]
Expected Result: Error message about message being too long
Actual Result: ____________
Status: ⬜ Pass / ⬜ Fail
```

### TC-004: Crisis Keyword Detection
```
Test ID: TC-004
Requirement: FR-5
Description: Crisis keywords trigger helpline response
Preconditions: App running
Test Steps:
  1. Type a message containing a crisis keyword
  2. Click Send
Test Data: "I don't want to live anymore"
Expected Result: Bot shows crisis response with helpline numbers (Lifeline: 13 11 14)
Actual Result: ____________
Status: ⬜ Pass / ⬜ Fail
```

### TC-005: Disclaimer Visibility
```
Test ID: TC-005
Requirement: FR-6
Description: Disclaimer message is visible to users
Preconditions: App running
Test Steps:
  1. Navigate to the chat page
  2. Look for a disclaimer/warning message
Test Data: N/A (visual inspection)
Expected Result: Disclaimer visible stating bot is AI and may not be accurate
Actual Result: ____________
Status: ⬜ Pass / ⬜ Fail
```

### TC-006: Message Styling
```
Test ID: TC-006
Requirement: NFR-2, NFR-3
Description: Messages display with correct colours and alignment
Preconditions: App running, at least one exchange completed
Test Steps:
  1. Send a message
  2. Wait for bot response
  3. Inspect visual styling
Test Data: "Test message"
Expected Result: User message = blue, right-aligned. Bot message = grey, left-aligned
Actual Result: ____________
Status: ⬜ Pass / ⬜ Fail
```

content.question: TC-004 tests crisis keyword detection. Why is this considered a critical test case?
options: Because it's the most difficult feature to code
Because it directly impacts user safety and wellbeing
Because it uses the most complex algorithm
Because it's the feature teachers will check first
content.explanation: Crisis keyword detection is critical because it directly affects user safety. If someone is in distress and types a crisis-related message, they need to see helpful resources immediately. A failure in this test case could have serious real-world consequences for vulnerable users.

content.text:
## Running Your Tests

### Step 1: Start Your Application

```bash
# Make sure virtual environment is active
source venv/bin/activate

# Run the app
python app.py
```

Wait for: `Running on http://0.0.0.0:5000`

### Step 2: Open the Application

1. Click the **Ports** tab in Codespaces
2. Click the globe icon 🌐 next to port 5000
3. Your chat interface should open

### Step 3: Execute Each Test Case

Go through TC-001 to TC-006 one by one:
1. Read the test steps
2. Perform the actions exactly as described
3. Compare the actual result to the expected result
4. Record Pass ✅ or Fail ❌

### Step 4: Document Your Results

Use this format to record your testing:

| Test ID | Status | Notes |
|---------|--------|---------|
| TC-001 | ✅ / ❌ | |
| TC-002 | ✅ / ❌ | |
| TC-003 | ✅ / ❌ | |
| TC-004 | ✅ / ❌ | |
| TC-005 | ✅ / ❌ | |
| TC-006 | ✅ / ❌ | |

content.description: Execute all 6 test cases (TC-001 to TC-006) on your chatbot. Record your results by listing each test ID, whether it passed or failed, and any notes about unexpected behaviour.
content.example_answer:
TC-001: PASS - Bot responded with 'Hello! How can I help you?' which is a relevant greeting.
TC-002: PASS - Got 'Please enter a message!' as expected.
TC-003: PASS - Got 'Message too long!' when I pasted 600 characters.
TC-004: PASS - Crisis response showed with Lifeline number 13 11 14.
TC-005: PASS - Disclaimer clearly visible at top saying 'This is an AI chatbot'.
TC-006: PASS - User messages blue on right, bot messages grey on left.

All tests passed! The chatbot meets all requirements.

content.text:
## Common Issues and Debugging

### Issue: TC-002 Fails (Empty message not caught)

**Possible Cause**: Missing validation in `/chat` route

**Solution**: Check your `app.py` has:
```python
if not user_message:
    return jsonify({'response': 'Please enter a message!'})
```

### Issue: TC-003 Fails (Long message not caught)

**Possible Cause**: Missing length validation

**Solution**: Check for:
```python
if len(user_message) > 500:
    return jsonify({'response': 'Message too long! Please keep it under 500 characters.'})
```

### Issue: TC-004 Fails (Crisis keywords not detected)

**Possible Cause**: Missing or incorrect keyword list

**Solution**: Ensure `CRISIS_KEYWORDS` list exists and `check_for_crisis()` function is called before getting bot response.

### Issue: "ModuleNotFoundError"

**Solution**:
```bash
source venv/bin/activate
pip install flask chatterbot chatterbot_corpus
```

### Issue: Port 5000 already in use

**Solution**:
```bash
lsof -i :5000
kill -9 <PID>
# Or change port in app.run(port=5001)
```

content.question: TC-002 fails - the app doesn't show an error when you send an empty message. What should you check FIRST?
options: Check if Bootstrap CSS is loading correctly
Check if the /chat route has input validation for empty messages
Clear the browser cache and try again
content.explanation: When input validation fails, the first place to check is the code that handles the input - in this case, the /chat route in app.py. You need to verify there's a check for empty messages that returns an error response.

content.text:
## Test Summary Report

After testing, create a summary in your README.md:

```markdown
## Testing

### User Acceptance Testing Results

Tested on: [DATE]
Tester: [YOUR NAME]

| Test ID | Description | Status |
|---------|-------------|--------|
| TC-001 | Normal message response | ✅ Pass |
| TC-002 | Empty message handling | ✅ Pass |
| TC-003 | Message length validation | ✅ Pass |
| TC-004 | Crisis keyword detection | ✅ Pass |
| TC-005 | Disclaimer visibility | ✅ Pass |
| TC-006 | Message styling | ✅ Pass |

**Summary**: All 6 test cases passed. The chatbot meets all functional and non-functional requirements.
```

> 💡 **Professional Practice**: Real software projects always include testing documentation. This shows others that you've verified your code works!

content.description: Reflect on the testing process: What bugs (if any) did you find during testing? How did you fix them? If all tests passed on the first try, describe one test case that you found most valuable and why.
content.example_answer: During testing, TC-003 initially failed because I had forgotten to add the message length check. The bot was accepting extremely long messages which could potentially crash the system. I fixed it by adding an if statement to check len(user_message) > 500 before processing. After the fix, I re-ran TC-003 and it passed. This experience taught me why systematic testing is important - I never would have thought to test very long messages without the test case.

content.text:
## Save Your Progress

After completing testing and updating your README with test results:

```bash
# Stop the server first (Ctrl+C)

git add .
git commit -m "docs: add UAT results to README"
git push
```

Notice we use `docs:` type because we're updating documentation with test results.

## ✅ Checklist: Before Moving On

- [ ] Understand what UAT is and why it matters
- [ ] Created/reviewed test cases TC-001 through TC-006
- [ ] Executed all test cases systematically
- [ ] Documented actual results for each test
- [ ] Fixed any bugs discovered during testing
- [ ] Added testing section to README.md
- [ ] All 6 test cases pass
- [ ] Committed and pushed your changes

> 👍 **Great work!** You've professionally tested your application. In the next lesson, we'll learn how to write automated tests and extend your chatbot further!

## Summary

| Concept | Key Takeaway |
|---------|--------------|
| **UAT** | Tests software from the user's perspective |
| **Test Case** | Formal document with steps, data, and expected results |
| **Functional Req** | What the system DOES (features) |
| **Non-Functional Req** | HOW the system performs (quality) |
| **Test Status** | Pass ✅ or Fail ❌ based on expected vs actual |
| **Documentation** | Always record test results professionally |

**Professional Tip**: In industry, UAT is often the final gate before software is released. If UAT fails, the software goes back to development!
